Name: JARS-Single-Case
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 5: Reporting Standards for N-of-1 Studies
Description: The JARS-Quant single-case table summarizes the psychology-specific reporting expectations for N-of-1 and related single-case experimental designs, drawing on SCRIBE 2016 for behavioral intervention contexts.
Aim: To improve clarity and replicability of single-case experimental studies by specifying how design structure, procedural changes, randomization, sequence completion, and raw outcome data should be reported.

Scope.Coverage: Single-case and N-of-1 experimental studies in psychology and behavioral intervention research, especially designs such as withdrawal-reversal, multiple-baseline, alternating-treatments, and changing-criterion studies.
Scope.Focus: Design type and phase structure, procedural changes, replication planning, randomization use, completed sequence reporting, stopping reasons, and raw participant-level outcomes.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for conventional group experiments or observational studies. This module is intended to be used alongside the JARS-Quant base table and relevant experimental modules.

primary.description: Appelbaum M, Cooper H, Kline RB, Mayo-Wilson E, Nezu AM, Rao SM. Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology: The APA Publications and Communications Board Task Force Report. American Psychologist. 2018. PMID: 29345484.

items.item_question: Does the manuscript describe the design type, phases, phase sequence, and any criteria for phase change?
items.item_question_background: Single-case designs are interpretable only when the logic of phases and their sequence are explicit, including whether changes were prespecified or data-driven.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the design type, phases, sequence, and any criteria for phase transitions.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe the design, including ... Design type ... Phases and phase sequence ... criteria for phase change.

items.item_name: Report procedural changes and planned replication
items.item_question: Does the manuscript describe procedural changes after study start and any planned replication?
items.item_question_background: Because single-case studies are highly procedural, post-start changes and replication plans materially affect how readers interpret evidential strength.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any procedural changes after study start and any statement about planned replication.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe any procedural changes that occurred during the course of the investigation ... Describe any planned replication.
source_page_refs.locator: Table 5 procedural changes and replication rows

items.item_name: Report randomization in single-case designs when used
items.item_question: If randomization was used, does the manuscript describe the randomization method and which study elements were randomized?
items.item_question_background: Randomization is optional in many single-case designs, so authors should be explicit when it is used and what exactly was randomized.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether randomization was used, the randomization method, and the randomized phases or conditions.

source_page_refs.quote: State whether randomization was used, and if so, describe the randomization method and the elements of the study that were randomized.

items.item_name: Report the completed sequence and stopping reasons
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the sequence actually completed for each participant, including the number of trials per session and any stopping points or reasons for stopping?
items.item_question_background: Single-case inference depends on the achieved sequence, not only the intended one, so readers need to know what was actually completed.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the achieved sequence, number of trials per session, and any stopping points or reasons for noncompletion.

source_page_refs.quote: Report for each participant the sequence actually completed ... State when participant(s) who did not complete the sequence stopped and the reason for stopping.

items.item_question: Does the manuscript report raw data for each participant and each target behavior or other outcome?
items.item_question_background: Raw outcome traces are foundational for evaluating single-case studies and enabling reanalysis or visual inspection.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether raw participant-level data are shown for each target behavior or outcome and how they are presented.

source_page_refs.quote: Report results for each participant, including raw data for each target behavior and other outcomes.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A behavioral intervention paper reports a multiple-baseline N-of-1 design, achieved phase sequence, randomization, and raw participant traces.
A single-case psychology article explicitly references SCRIBE and provides participant-level outcome data.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A standard group randomized trial is not a JARS-Single-Case manuscript.
A meta-analysis or observational cohort with no phase-based single-case design should not map here.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts reporting N-of-1 or other single-case experimental designs.
